**William Clark Styron Jr.** (June 11, 1925 – November 1, 2006) was an American [novelist](/wiki/Novelist "Novelist") and [essayist](/wiki/Essayist "Essayist") who won major literary awards for his work, including the [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction") in 1968 for [*The Confessions of Nat Turner*](/wiki/The_Confessions_of_Nat_Turner "The Confessions of Nat Turner").[[1]](#cite_note-dies-1)

## Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life")]

Styron was born in the [Hilton Village](/wiki/Hilton_Village "Hilton Village") historic district[[2]](#cite_note-2) of [Newport News, Virginia](/wiki/Newport_News,_Virginia "Newport News, Virginia"), the son of Pauline Margaret (Abraham) and William Clark Styron.[[1]](#cite_note-dies-1) His birthplace was less than a hundred miles from the site of [Nat Turner's slave rebellion](/wiki/Nat_Turner%27s_slave_rebellion "Nat Turner's slave rebellion"), the inspiration for Styron's most famous and controversial novel.

Styron's mother was from the [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") while his father was a [Southern](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") [liberal](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism"), laying out broad racial perspectives in the household. Styron's father, a shipyard engineer, suffered [clinical depression](/wiki/Clinical_depression "Clinical depression"), as would later Styron himself. In 1939, at age 14, Styron lost his mother after her decade-long battle with [breast cancer](/wiki/Breast_cancer "Breast cancer").

Styron attended public school in Warwick County, first at [Hilton School](/wiki/Hilton_Elementary_School_(Newport_News,_Virginia) "Hilton Elementary School (Newport News, Virginia)") and then at Morrison High School (now known as [Warwick High School](/wiki/Warwick_High_School_(Newport_News) "Warwick High School (Newport News)")) for two years, until his father sent him to [Christchurch School](/wiki/Christchurch_School "Christchurch School"), an [Episcopal](/wiki/Episcopal_Church_in_the_United_States_of_America "Episcopal Church in the United States of America") [college-preparatory school](/wiki/College-preparatory_school "College-preparatory school") in the [Tidewater region of Virginia](/wiki/Tidewater_region_of_Virginia "Tidewater region of Virginia"). Styron once said "of all the schools I attended ... only Christchurch ever commanded something more than mere respect—which is to say, my true and abiding affection."[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On graduation, Styron enrolled in [Davidson College](/wiki/Davidson_College "Davidson College")[[4]](#cite_note-Eric_Homberger-4) and joined [Phi Delta Theta](/wiki/Phi_Delta_Theta "Phi Delta Theta"). By age eighteen he was reading the writers who would have a lasting influence on his own work, especially [Thomas Wolfe](/wiki/Thomas_Wolfe "Thomas Wolfe").[[4]](#cite_note-Eric_Homberger-4) Styron transferred to [Duke University](/wiki/Duke_University "Duke University") in 1943 as a part of the [U.S. Navy](/wiki/United_States_Navy "United States Navy") and [Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps") [V-12 program](/wiki/V-12_Navy_College_Training_Program "V-12 Navy College Training Program") aimed at fast-tracking officer candidates by enrolling them simultaneously in basic training and bachelor's degree programs. There he published his first fiction, a short story heavily influenced by [William Faulkner](/wiki/William_Faulkner "William Faulkner"), in an anthology of student work [*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]. Styron published several short stories in the university literary magazine, *The Archive*, between 1944 and 1946.[[5]](#cite_note-StyronPapers-5) Though Styron was made a [lieutenant](/wiki/Lieutenant "Lieutenant") in the [U.S. Marine Corps](/wiki/United_States_Marine_Corps "United States Marine Corps"), the Japanese surrendered before his ship left [San Francisco](/wiki/San_Francisco "San Francisco"). After the war, he returned to full-time studies at Duke and completed his Bachelor of Arts (B.A.) in [English](/wiki/English_studies "English studies") in 1947.[[5]](#cite_note-StyronPapers-5)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

After graduation, Styron took an editing position with [McGraw-Hill](/wiki/McGraw-Hill "McGraw-Hill") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). Styron later recalled the misery of this work in an autobiographical passage of *Sophie’s Choice*. After provoking his employers into firing him, he set about writing his first novel in earnest. Three years later, he published the novel, *[Lie Down in Darkness](/wiki/Lie_Down_in_Darkness_(novel) "Lie Down in Darkness (novel)")* (1951), the story of a dysfunctional Virginia family. The novel received overwhelming critical acclaim. For this novel, Styron received the [Rome Prize](/wiki/Rome_Prize "Rome Prize"), awarded by the [American Academy in Rome](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Rome "American Academy in Rome") and the [American Academy of Arts and Letters](/wiki/American_Academy_of_Arts_and_Letters "American Academy of Arts and Letters").

### Military service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Military service")]

His recall into the military due to the [Korean War](/wiki/Korean_War "Korean War") prevented him from immediately accepting the Rome Prize. Styron joined the Marine Corps, but was discharged in 1952 for eye problems. However, he was to transform his experience at [Camp Lejeune](/wiki/Camp_Lejeune "Camp Lejeune"), North Carolina into his short novel, *[The Long March](/wiki/The_Long_March_(novel) "The Long March (novel)")*, published serially the following year. This was adapted for the *[Playhouse 90](/wiki/Playhouse_90 "Playhouse 90")* episode "The Long March" in 1958.

### Travels in Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Travels in Europe")]

Styron spent an extended period in Europe. In [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), he became friends with writers [Romain Gary](/wiki/Romain_Gary "Romain Gary"), [George Plimpton](/wiki/George_Plimpton "George Plimpton"), [Peter Matthiessen](/wiki/Peter_Matthiessen "Peter Matthiessen"), [James Baldwin](/wiki/James_Baldwin "James Baldwin"), [James Jones](/wiki/James_Jones_(author) "James Jones (author)") and [Irwin Shaw](/wiki/Irwin_Shaw "Irwin Shaw"), among others. In 1953, the group founded the magazine *[The Paris Review](/wiki/The_Paris_Review "The Paris Review")*, which became a celebrated literary journal.[[4]](#cite_note-Eric_Homberger-4)

The year 1953 was eventful for Styron in another way. Finally able to take advantage of his Rome Prize, he traveled to Italy, where he became friends with [Truman Capote](/wiki/Truman_Capote "Truman Capote"). At the American Academy, he renewed an acquaintance with a young [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") poet, [Rose Burgunder](/wiki/Rose_Burgunder "Rose Burgunder"), to whom he had been introduced the previous fall at [Johns Hopkins University](/wiki/Johns_Hopkins_University "Johns Hopkins University"). They were married in Rome in the spring of 1953.

Some of Styron's experiences during this period inspired his third published book *[Set This House on Fire](/wiki/Set_This_House_on_Fire "Set This House on Fire")* (1960), a novel about intellectual American [expatriates](/wiki/Expatriate "Expatriate") on the Amalfi coast of Italy. The novel received mixed reviews in the United States, although its publisher considered it successful in terms of sales. In Europe its translation into French achieved best-seller status, far outselling the American edition.

## Nat Turner controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Nat Turner controversy")]

Styron's next two novels, published between 1967 and 1979, sparked much controversy. Feeling wounded by his first truly harsh reviews[[6]](#cite_note-6) for *Set This House on Fire*, Styron spent the years after its publication researching and writing his next novel, the fictitious memoirs of the historical [Nathaniel "Nat" Turner](/wiki/Nat_Turner "Nat Turner"), a slave who led a slave rebellion in 1831.

During the 1960s, Styron became an eyewitness to another time of rebellion in the United States, living and writing at the heart of that turbulent decade, a time highlighted by the [counterculture revolution](/wiki/Counterculture_of_the_1960s "Counterculture of the 1960s") with its political struggle, civil unrest, and racial tension. The public response to this social upheaval was furious and intense: battle lines were being drawn. In 1968, Styron signed the "[Writers and Editors War Tax Protest](/wiki/Writers_and_Editors_War_Tax_Protest "Writers and Editors War Tax Protest")" pledge, a vow refusing to pay taxes as a protest against the Vietnam War.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In this atmosphere of dissent, many[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] had criticized Styron's friend James Baldwin for his novel *[Another Country](/wiki/Another_Country_(novel) "Another Country (novel)")*, published in 1962. Among the criticisms was outrage over a black author choosing a white woman as the [protagonist](/wiki/Protagonist "Protagonist") in a story that tells of her involvement with a black man. Baldwin was Styron's house guest for several months following the critical storm generated by *Another Country*. During that time, he read early drafts of Styron's new novel, and predicted that Styron's book would face even harsher scrutiny than *Another Country*. "Bill's going to catch it from both sides", he told an interviewer immediately following the 1967 publication of *[The Confessions of Nat Turner](/wiki/The_Confessions_of_Nat_Turner "The Confessions of Nat Turner")*.

Baldwin's prediction was correct, and despite public defenses of Styron by leading artists of the time, including Baldwin and [Ralph Ellison](/wiki/Ralph_Ellison "Ralph Ellison"), numerous other black critics reviled Styron's portrayal of Turner as racist stereotyping. The historian and critic [John Henrik Clarke](/wiki/John_Henrik_Clarke "John Henrik Clarke") edited and contributed to a polemical anthology, *William Styron's Nat Turner: Ten Black Writers Respond*, published in 1968 by Beacon Press. Particularly controversial was a passage in which Turner fantasizes about [raping](/wiki/Rape "Rape") a white woman. Several critics pointed to this as a dangerous perpetuation of a traditional Southern justification for [lynching](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching"). Styron also writes of a situation where Turner and another slave boy have a homosexual encounter while alone in the woods. Despite the controversy, the novel was a runaway critical and financial success, and won both the 1968 [Pulitzer Prize for Fiction](/wiki/Pulitzer_Prize_for_Fiction "Pulitzer Prize for Fiction")[[8]](#cite_note-8) and the [William Dean Howells Medal](/wiki/William_Dean_Howells_Medal "William Dean Howells Medal") in 1970.

## Benjamin Reid controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Benjamin Reid controversy")]

In the early 1960s, Styron became a mentor to prisoner Benjamin Reid, who in 1957 had beaten a woman to death with a hammer in a botched robbery attempt. Through his writings and advocacy, Styron successfully helped to have Reid's death sentence commuted in 1962. In 1970, Reid escaped prison before his scheduled parole and kidnapped and raped a woman.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## *Sophie's Choice*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Sophie's Choice")]

Styron's next novel, *[Sophie's Choice](/wiki/Sophie%27s_Choice_(novel) "Sophie's Choice (novel)")* (1979),
also generated significant controversy, in part due to Styron's decision to portray a non-[Jewish](/wiki/Jew "Jew") victim of [Nazism](/wiki/Nazism "Nazism") and in part due to its explicit sexuality and profanity. It was banned in South Africa, censored in the Soviet Union, and banned in [Communist Poland](/wiki/Polish_People%27s_Republic "Polish People's Republic") for "its unflinching portrait of Polish anti-Semitism."[[10]](#cite_note-10) It has also been banned in some high schools in the United States.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

The novel tells the story of Sophie (a Polish [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") who survives [Auschwitz](/wiki/Auschwitz "Auschwitz")), Nathan (her brilliant Jewish lover who has paranoid schizophrenia), and Stingo (a Southern transplant in post-World War II-[Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") who falls in love with Sophie). The novel borrowed from Styron's interest in the psychological links between the Holocaust and American Slavery.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It won the 1980 [National Book Award](/wiki/National_Book_Award "National Book Award")[[13]](#cite_note-nba1980-13)[[n 1]](#cite_note-14) and was a nationwide bestseller. A 1982 [film version](/wiki/Sophie%27s_Choice_(film) "Sophie's Choice (film)") was nominated for five [Academy Awards](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"), with [Meryl Streep](/wiki/Meryl_Streep "Meryl Streep") winning the [Academy Award for Best Actress](/wiki/Academy_Award_for_Best_Actress "Academy Award for Best Actress") for her portrayal of Sophie. [Kevin Kline](/wiki/Kevin_Kline "Kevin Kline") and [Peter MacNicol](/wiki/Peter_MacNicol "Peter MacNicol") played Nathan and Stingo, respectively.

## *Darkness Visible*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Darkness Visible")]

Styron's readership expanded with the publication of [*Darkness Visible*](/wiki/Darkness_Visible_(memoir) "Darkness Visible (memoir)") in 1990. This memoir, which began as a magazine article, chronicles the author's descent into [depression](/wiki/Major_depression "Major depression") and his near-fatal night of "despair beyond despair".[[14]](#cite_note-15) It is a first-hand account of a major depressive episode and challenged the modern taboo on acknowledging such issues. The memoir's goals included increasing knowledge and decreasing stigmatization of major depressive disorders and suicide. It explored the [phenomenology](/wiki/Phenomenology_(psychology) "Phenomenology (psychology)") of the disease among those with depression, their loved ones, and the general public as well.

Earlier, in December 1989, Styron had written an op-ed for *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* responding to the disappointment and mystification among scholars about the apparent suicide of [Primo Levi](/wiki/Primo_Levi "Primo Levi"), the remarkable Italian writer who survived the [Nazi death camps](/wiki/Nazi_death_camps "Nazi death camps"), but apparently had depression in his final years. Reportedly, it was the public's unsympathetic response to Levi's death that impelled Styron to take a more active role as an advocate for educating the public about the nature of depression and the role it played in mental health and suicide.[[4]](#cite_note-Eric_Homberger-4)

Styron noted in an article for *[Vanity Fair](/wiki/Vanity_Fair_(magazine) "Vanity Fair (magazine)")* that

> the pain of severe depression is quite unimaginable to those who have not suffered it, and it kills in many instances because its anguish can no longer be borne. The prevention of many suicides will continue to be hindered until there is a general awareness of the nature of this pain. Through the healing process of time—and through medical intervention or hospitalization in many cases—most people survive depression, which may be its only blessing; but to the tragic legion who are compelled to destroy themselves there should be no more reproof attached than to the victims of terminal cancer.[[15]](#cite_note-Vanity_Fair_Darkness_visible-16)

## Later work and acclaim

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Later work and acclaim")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Bill_Styron_in_his_West_Chop_writing_room_on_Martha%27s_Vineyard_-_August_1989.jpg/250px-Bill_Styron_in_his_West_Chop_writing_room_on_Martha%27s_Vineyard_-_August_1989.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Styron_in_his_West_Chop_writing_room_on_Martha%27s_Vineyard_-_August_1989.jpg)

Styron in 1989

Styron was awarded the [St. Louis Literary Award](/wiki/St._Louis_Literary_Award "St. Louis Literary Award") from the [Saint Louis University](/wiki/Saint_Louis_University_(United_States) "Saint Louis University (United States)") Library Associates.[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[17]](#cite_note-18)

Styron was awarded the [Prix mondial Cino Del Duca](/wiki/Prix_mondial_Cino_Del_Duca "Prix mondial Cino Del Duca") in 1985.

His short story "Shadrach" was filmed in 1998, under the [same title](/wiki/Shadrach_(film) "Shadrach (film)"). It was co-directed by his daughter [Susanna Styron](/wiki/Susanna_Styron "Susanna Styron").

Other works published during his lifetime include the play *In the Clap Shack* (1973), and a collection of his nonfiction, *This Quiet Dust* (1982).

French president [François Mitterrand](/wiki/Fran%C3%A7ois_Mitterrand "François Mitterrand") invited Styron to his first Presidential inauguration, and later made him a Commander of the [Legion of Honor](/wiki/Legion_of_Honor "Legion of Honor").[[18]](#cite_note-19) In 1993, Styron was awarded the [National Medal of Arts](/wiki/National_Medal_of_Arts "National Medal of Arts").[[19]](#cite_note-20)

In 2002 an opera by [Nicholas Maw](/wiki/Nicholas_Maw "Nicholas Maw") based on *Sophie's Choice* premièred at the [Royal Opera House](/wiki/Royal_Opera_House "Royal Opera House") in [Covent Garden](/wiki/Covent_Garden "Covent Garden"), [London](/wiki/London "London"). Maw wrote the [libretto](/wiki/Libretto "Libretto") and composed the music. He had approached Styron about writing the libretto, but Styron declined. Later the opera received a new production by stage director Markus Bothe at the [Deutsche Oper Berlin](/wiki/Deutsche_Oper_Berlin "Deutsche Oper Berlin") and the [Volksoper Wien](/wiki/Volksoper_Wien "Volksoper Wien"), and had its North American premiere at the [Washington National Opera](/wiki/Washington_National_Opera "Washington National Opera") in October 2006.[[20]](#cite_note-NYT-Maw-21)

A collection of Styron's papers and records is housed at the Rubenstein Library, Duke University.[[5]](#cite_note-StyronPapers-5)

In 1996 William Styron received the 1st Fitzgerald Award on the centenary of F. Scott Fitzgerald's birth. The F. Scott Fitzgerald Award for Achievement in American Literature award is given annually in Rockville Maryland, the city where Fitzgerald, his wife, and his daughter are buried, as part of the F. Scott Fitzgerald Literary Festival. In 1988 he was awarded the [Edward MacDowell Medal](/wiki/Edward_MacDowell_Medal "Edward MacDowell Medal").[[21]](#cite_note-22)

He was a Charter member of the [Fellowship of Southern Writers](/wiki/Fellowship_of_Southern_Writers "Fellowship of Southern Writers").

### Port Warwick street names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Port Warwick street names")]

The [Port Warwick](/wiki/Port_Warwick "Port Warwick") neighborhood of Newport News, Virginia, was named after the fictional city in Styron's *Lie Down in Darkness*. The neighborhood describes itself as a "mixed-use [new urbanism](/wiki/New_urbanism "New urbanism") development." The most prominent feature of Port Warwick is William Styron Square along with its two main [boulevards](/wiki/Boulevard "Boulevard"), Loftis Boulevard and Nat Turner Boulevard, named after characters in Styron's novels. Styron himself was appointed to design a naming system for Port Warwick, deciding to "honor great American writers", resulting in [Philip Roth](/wiki/Philip_Roth "Philip Roth") Street, [Thomas Wolfe](/wiki/Thomas_Wolfe "Thomas Wolfe") Street, [Flannery O'Connor](/wiki/Flannery_O%27Connor "Flannery O'Connor") Street, [Herman Melville](/wiki/Herman_Melville "Herman Melville") Avenue and others.[[22]](#cite_note-23)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=William_Styron&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

In 1985, he had his first serious bout with [depression](/wiki/Major_depression "Major depression"). Once he recovered from his illness, Styron was able to write the memoir *[Darkness Visible](/wiki/Darkness_Visible_(memoir) "Darkness Visible (memoir)")* (1990), the work for which he became best known during the last two decades of his life.

While doing a fellowship at the [American Academy in Rome](/wiki/American_Academy_in_Rome "American Academy in Rome"), Styron renewed a passing acquaintance with young [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore") poet Rose Burgunder. They married in Rome in the spring of 1953. Together, they had four children: daughter Susanna Styron is a film director; daughter Paola is an internationally acclaimed modern dancer; daughter [Alexandra](/wiki/Alexandra_Styron "Alexandra Styron") is a writer, known for the 2001 novel *All The Finest Girls* and 2011 memoir *Reading My Father: A Memoir*; and son Thomas is a professor of [clinical psychology](/wiki/Clinical_psychology "Clinical psychology") at [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University").

Styron died from [pneumonia](/wiki/Pneumonia "Pneumonia") on November 1, 2006, at age 81, on [Martha's Vineyard](/wiki/Martha%27s_Vineyard "Martha's Vineyard"). He is buried at [West Chop](/wiki/West_Chop "West Chop") Cemetery in [Vineyard Haven](/wiki/Vineyard_Haven "Vineyard Haven"), [Dukes County](/wiki/Dukes_County "Dukes County"), [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts").[[23]](#cite_note-24)
